title: Anti-Money Laundering Agent
problem: AML/compliance teams spend hours manually reviewing suspicious-activity emails, cross-checking customer records, and writing closure decisions — with high regulatory deadline pressure.
solution: Email-triggered agent runs a parallel investigation (public profile, bank account lookup, AML policy retrieval), drafts a closure decision, sends to human for review, then commits the closure to the portal on approval.
whoItsFor: AML Officers, Fraud & Risk Operations Analysts
workflowSteps: Trigger fires on new AML notification email
Parallel subagents: (1) research public profile, (2) find matching bank account, (3) retrieve AML policy docs from SharePoint
Coordinator merges typed results into a draft closure decision
Sends draft + evidence to human reviewer via portal
If approved: logs decision, closes account in portal, archives
If rejected: records dissent, escalates to senior compliance officer
keyInsights: Classic parallel-subagent coordinator pattern. No account is closed without explicit human approval — HITL is a regulatory requirement, not just a safety preference. The typed merge contract across three parallel subagents is what makes this auditable.

relatedExams.why: Agents and Workflows — parallel subagent coordination
Governance, Safety & Risk Management — regulated industry HITL requirements

mermaidDiagram:
flowchart LR
  A([AML Alert Email]) --> B[Coordinator]
  B --> C1[Public Profile Search]
  B --> C2[Bank Account Lookup]
  B --> C3[AML Policy Docs]
  C1 & C2 & C3 --> D[Merge Typed Evidence]
  D --> E[Draft Closure Decision]
  E --> F[HITL Reviewer Portal]
  F -->|Approved| G([Close Account + Audit Log])
  F -->|Rejected| H([Escalate to Senior Officer])
architectureNotes: Multiple risk-check agents run in parallel (velocity, watchlist, typology) against the same transaction record. A scoring aggregator collects their outputs and applies a threshold rule: score ≥ threshold triggers a SAR draft; score between the soft and hard limits routes to a human analyst for review. Parallelism cuts latency vs. sequential checks; the HITL layer satisfies regulatory oversight requirements.
